title: British Comedy
subtitle: Panel shows, sitcoms, game shows, news comedy, and annual specials. Tap a category to filter.

sections.name: I. Already Loved
sections.desc: Your established British comedy pillars. Listed for tracking and rewatch.

items.title: QI
items.seasons: Stephen Fry hosted A-M (2003-15); Sandi Toksvig from N (2016-)
items.pitch: British panel show about quite interesting things. The standard against which all panel shows are measured. You've watched every episode.

critics.quote: BBC's most-acclaimed panel format. Continues to win awards across hosts.

items.title: 8 Out of 10 Cats Does Countdown
items.seasons: Carr hosts; Sean Lock and Jon Richardson team captains (Lock until 2021)
items.pitch: Hybrid of 8 Out of 10 Cats and Countdown. Susie Dent in Dictionary Corner. Specials with an extended cast (David Mitchell, Bob Mortimer, Joe Wilkinson) are reliably the best episodes.

critics.quote: Considered Channel 4's flagship comedy panel show of the 2010s-2020s.

items.title: Would I Lie to You?
items.pitch: Rob Brydon hosts. David Mitchell and Lee Mack are team captains. Players try to deceive each other about whether stories from their lives are true.

critics.quote: BAFTA-winning. Considered alongside QI and Taskmaster as the British panel-show triumvirate.

items.title: Taskmaster
items.seasons: Greg Davies hosts; Alex Horne is his assistant. Each series 5 contestants × 10 episodes. New Year's Treat specials are highly regarded.
items.pitch: Five comedians complete absurd tasks set by Alex Horne. Greg Davies judges them. The pre-recorded task films are the reason the show works.

critics.quote: Won BAFTA for Best Comedy Entertainment Programme. Format licensed to ~12 countries.

items.title: Vicious
items.pitch: Ian McKellen and Derek Jacobi as an aging gay couple in London who have lived together for nearly 50 years and bicker constantly. Theatrical leads carrying a traditional sitcom format.

critics.quote: Critical reception was mixed (some called it old-fashioned). Audience devotion strong.

items.title: Pointless
items.pitch: Alexander Armstrong hosts. Richard Osman was his sidekick (left 2022; replaced by rotating co-hosts). Contestants try to find the LEAST popular correct answers. The Armstrong/Osman chemistry was the engine.

critics.quote: BAFTA-winning. Long the highest-rated daytime quiz show in the UK.

items.title: Only Connect
items.pitch: Victoria Coren Mitchell hosts. Teams of three find connections between obscure things. Considered the most cerebrally demanding quiz on UK television.

critics.quote: Cult-to-mainstream trajectory across its run. Considered the brain-quiz benchmark.

items.title: University Challenge
items.seasons: Bamber Gascoigne 1962-87; Jeremy Paxman 1994-2023; Amol Rajan 2023-
items.pitch: Universities compete in cerebral quiz. The longest-running British quiz on television.

critics.quote: Cultural institution. Paxman era considered peak by many; Rajan era still establishing itself.

items.title: Big Fat Quiz of the Year
items.seasons: Annual since 2004; spin-offs include Big Fat Quiz of the Decade, Big Fat Anniversary Quiz
items.pitch: Jimmy Carr hosts. Three teams of two comedians answer questions about the year just past. The David Mitchell + Jonathan Ross + Russell Brand episodes are widely considered the format's peak.

critics.quote: Cult New Year's-week tradition. The 2008-2014 stretch is the era most cited as classic.

items.title: 8 Out of 10 Cats
items.pitch: Topical news-driven panel format. Without the Countdown structural anchor it didn't land for you. Listed for completeness.

critics.quote: Spawned the more-loved Cats Does Countdown spinoff in 2012.

sections.name: II. High Priority — Panel Shows
sections.desc: Direct fits given your panel-show love. Comedian-led, multi-host chemistry.

items.title: Have I Got News for You
items.whyPriority: Longest-running British panel show. Ian Hislop and Paul Merton anchor as team captains since 1990. Rotating guest hosts. The structural anchor is news-of-the-week, but Merton's free-association is what carries it.
items.pitch: Two teams of two compete on news of the week. Hislop is sharp and prepared; Merton goes off on tangents. Hosts rotate.

critics.quote: BAFTA-winning multiple times. Considered the British satirical-news panel template.

items.title: Mock the Week
items.seasons: All ended 2022. Dara Ó Briain hosted entire run.
items.whyPriority: Faster, comedian-heavier than HIGNFY. Hugh Dennis and Andy Parsons were regulars; rotating guests. The Scenes We'd Like to See round is the format hook.
items.pitch: Six comedians joke about the week's news. Set-pieces include topical questions and improv-style scenes.

critics.quote: Cancelled after BBC budget review. Considered the comedian-developer show — Romesh Ranganathan, Ed Gamble, James Acaster all came through.

items.title: Mock the Week-style alternative: The Mash Report
items.pitch: Nish Kumar hosts. Daily-Show-flavored news satire with British sensibility. Cancelled by BBC, revived by Dave as Late Night Mash.

critics.quote: Politically pointed; cancellation was controversial.

items.title: Cats Does Countdown — Specials
items.whyPriority: Christmas Specials and the recurring David Mitchell + Joe Wilkinson + Bob Mortimer guest configurations are widely considered the show's peak.
items.pitch: If you like the main series, the Christmas Specials and the recurring Mitchell/Wilkinson combinations are the canonical episodes.

items.title: Taskmaster — New Year's Treat
items.whyPriority: Annual New Year's Treat specials feature different lineups (often celebrities outside comedy). Widely regarded as the strongest specials in any UK panel-show franchise.
items.pitch: Annual variants where five new contestants do tasks. Self-contained.

sections.name: III. High Priority — Sitcoms
sections.desc: Theatrical/character-led, smart-witted, traditional sitcom craft. Explicitly avoids cringe-driven discomfort.

items.title: Yes Minister / Yes, Prime Minister
items.seasons: Yes Minister 1980-84 (3 series); Yes, PM 1986-88 (2 series)
items.whyPriority: Highest-priority sitcom recommendation. Nigel Hawthorne as Sir Humphrey Appleby is among the great comic-dramatic performances on TV. Sorkin-adjacent — three-character dialogue dance about how government actually works. Direct fit for your West Wing pillar.
items.pitch: A new Cabinet minister tries to enact policy; his Permanent Secretary gently sabotages him to preserve civil-service interests.

critics.quote: Won BAFTA Best Comedy three times. Margaret Thatcher's stated favorite show — she once wrote a sketch with Hawthorne for a charity event.

items.title: Blackadder
items.seasons: Series 1 (1983) is widely considered the weakest; Series 2-4 (1986-89) are the canonical run. Series 4 (Blackadder Goes Forth, WWI trenches) is widely cited as the masterpiece.
items.whyPriority: Rowan Atkinson at his peak. Each series transplants Edmund Blackadder to a different historical era. Series 4's final episode (the men go over the top in WWI) is one of the most-praised endings in British TV.
items.pitch: Edmund Blackadder schemes through history — Wars of the Roses, Elizabethan court, Regency, WWI trenches. Each series 6 episodes.

critics.quote: Voted #2 in BFI's 100 Greatest British Television Programmes. Series 4 finale routinely cited among the great moments in British TV.

items.title: Father Ted
items.seasons: Complete (Dermot Morgan died after Series 3 was filmed). Christmas special is canonical.
items.whyPriority: Three priests on a remote Irish island. Linehan-written. Three short perfect seasons. Tonally exact match for your Black Books loves (Linehan also).
items.pitch: Father Ted Crilly, Father Dougal McGuire, and Father Jack Hackett were exiled to a parish on Craggy Island for various ecclesiastical sins.

critics.quote: Won BAFTA Best Comedy. Routinely cited among the greatest sitcoms of British television.

items.title: Fawlty Towers
items.seasons: Complete (1975 + 1979). Cleese refused to make more.
items.whyPriority: John Cleese as Basil Fawlty. Twelve episodes, structurally perfect. Among the most-cited British sitcoms ever made. Note: occasional cringe content but it's farce-cringe (someone embarrasses *themselves* to the audience), not cringe-comedy in the modern Office sense.
items.pitch: Basil Fawlty runs a small hotel in Torquay. He hates the guests. Things go wrong.

critics.quote: BFI's #1 Greatest British Television Programme of all time (2000 poll).

items.title: Black Books
items.pitch: Cross-listed in Comedy TV. Already on your radar. Bernard Black runs a London bookshop he hates.

critics.quote: Won BAFTA Best Sitcom.

items.title: Back
items.whyPriority: Per your request. David Mitchell and Robert Webb's first scripted reunion since Peep Show. Dark comedy, no cringe — much closer to your sensibility. Mitchell plays a man whose long-lost foster brother shows up to take over his late father's pub.
items.pitch: Stephen takes over his father's pub. His foster brother Andrew shows up — charming, unsettling, possibly a sociopath.

critics.quote: Critical favorite. Considered the strongest Mitchell/Webb scripted work since Peep Show, with a more mature register.

sections.name: IV. Medium Priority — Sitcoms
sections.desc: Worth flagging. Includes the Mitchell/Webb cringe-warning entry.

items.title: Peep Show
items.seasons: Complete (ended 2015). Jesse Armstrong went on to create Succession.
items.whyPriority: Per your request. CRINGE WARNING: Peep Show is relentlessly cringe-driven — but structurally singular. Every shot is first-person POV with internal monologue voiceover. Mitchell + Webb at their best. If you can tolerate the cringe (it functions differently because of the POV device), this is a TV-history-grade work.
items.pitch: Mark and Jeremy share a London flat. Mark is a buttoned-up loan officer; Jeremy is an unemployed musician. We hear their private thoughts throughout.

critics.quote: Two BAFTAs. Considered one of the greatest British sitcoms of the 21st century — including by people who otherwise don't tolerate cringe comedy.

items.title: Keeping Up Appearances
items.whyPriority: Patricia Routledge as Hyacinth Bucket-pronounced-Bouquet. Broader than your usual lane but warmly written. Routledge's performance is the show.
items.pitch: A suburban Englishwoman desperately tries to project upper-class respectability while her embarrassing relatives keep undermining her.

critics.quote: Long ratings hit; Routledge's performance now considered classic.

items.title: As Time Goes By
items.whyPriority: Judi Dench and Geoffrey Palmer. Two former lovers separated by the Korean War reunite in middle age. Slower, romantic, character-led. Dench-anchored craft.
items.pitch: Jean and Lionel were briefly engaged in the 1950s. Decades later they meet again by chance.

critics.quote: Ran nine series. Considered a benchmark for the gentle-romantic-sitcom register.

items.title: Spaced
items.dir: Edgar Wright / Simon Pegg / Jessica Hynes
items.pitch: Cross-listed in Comedy TV. Edgar Wright's first directing project. Genre-pastiche per episode — the structural ancestor of Community.

critics.quote: Established Wright/Pegg/Frost as a unit.

sections.name: V. Medium Priority — Game Shows
sections.desc: Direct fits given your Pointless / Only Connect / University Challenge loves.

items.title: Richard Osman's House of Games
items.whyPriority: Per your interest in more Osman. Four celebrity panelists compete across a five-day week. Games rotate. Osman's banter is the show. Direct fit for your Pointless love.
items.pitch: A weeklong celebrity quiz show with Osman as host and a rotating set of word, number, and trivia games.

critics.quote: BBC daytime hit. Considered the best Osman-only vehicle since Pointless.

items.title: The Chase
items.whyPriority: Bradley Walsh hosts. Five elite quizzers (the Chasers) — Anne Hegerty, Mark Labbett, Shaun Wallace, Paul Sinha, Jenny Ryan, Darragh Ennis. Contestants try to outrun a Chaser to win prize money. Walsh's hosting is the warm-and-funny variable that makes it work.
items.pitch: Contestants build a cash bank; an elite quizzer tries to catch them on a quiz ladder.

critics.quote: Top-rated daytime quiz in the UK for over a decade. Spawned multiple international versions.

items.title: Mastermind
items.seasons: Magnus Magnusson 1972-97; John Humphrys 2003-21; Clive Myrie 2021-
items.whyPriority: The original specialist-subject quiz. Two rounds: one on a chosen specialist subject, one on general knowledge. Myrie era considered an excellent revival.
items.pitch: Contestants face two timed quiz rounds in the iconic black chair. 'I've started, so I'll finish' is the catchphrase.

critics.quote: Cultural institution. Format licensed worldwide.

items.title: Countdown
items.whyPriority: The parent format of Cats Does Countdown. Words and numbers puzzles. Susie Dent is a constant. Quieter than the Carr-hosted spinoff but the structural anchor.
items.pitch: Contestants form longest-possible words from random letters and reach a target number from a set of randomly-drawn numbers. Daytime, gentle.

critics.quote: Channel 4's first-ever broadcast was Countdown. Cultural institution.

items.title: Only Connect — Specials
items.whyPriority: Christmas specials and themed series. Coren Mitchell's hosting register adapts well to special formats.
items.pitch: Holiday and themed specials are sprinkled across the regular series.

items.title: Outsiders
items.pitch: David Mitchell hosts. Comedians compete in survival challenges in a forest. Tonally adjacent to Taskmaster.

critics.quote: Promising start; reviewed warmly.

items.title: Would I Lie to You? — Specials & Best Of
items.pitch: Children in Need and Comic Relief specials, plus regular Best-Of compilations. The Bob Mortimer episodes are routinely the most-rewatched in the format.

items.title: Cats Does Countdown — Anniversary specials
items.pitch: 10th-anniversary best-ofs and themed compilations are circulating.

items.title: Big Fat Quiz of the Decade / Big Fat Quiz of Everything
items.pitch: Decade-spanning variants of Big Fat Quiz of the Year. Same panel show DNA, broader scope.

critics.quote: Less consistent than the annual; the 2014 Decade was the most-praised.

items.title: Detectorists
items.pitch: Cross-listed in Comedy TV. Mackenzie Crook's gentle metal-detecting comedy. The closest tonal match in modern British TV to your Vicious-and-warm-ensemble overlap zone.

critics.quote: Won BAFTA across all three series.

items.title: Inside No. 9
items.pitch: Cross-listed in Horror TV. Anthology — each episode self-contained 30-minute story that twists. Comedy and horror co-exist.

critics.quote: BAFTA-winning. Considered one of the most inventive British shows of its era.
